User experience in a kinect-based conducting system for visualization of 
musical structure☆ 
Joyce Horn Fonteles a, Maria Andréia Formico Rodrigues b,* 
a Programa de Pós-Graduaç˜ao em Informática Aplicada (PPGIA), Universidade de Fortaleza (UNIFOR), Brazil 
b Programa de Pós-Graduaç˜ao em Informática Aplicada (PPGIA), Universidade de Fortaleza (UNIFOR), Av. Washington Soares 1321, J(30), 60811-905 Fortaleza, CE, 
Brazil   
A R T I C L E  I N F O   
Keywords: 
Gestural control 
Conducting system 
Visualization 
Particle systems 
Interaction 
UX 
A B S T R A C T   
There is a potential for immersive tools that can teach some basic aspects of conducting in a constructive way. 
This work presents a Kinect-based conducting system that uses a gestural interface for visualization of musical 
structures, aimed towards educational purpose. The system provides a solution that enables students to practice 
conducting without the actual need of the physical presence of an orchestra or choir. Visualization of melodic 
and harmonic structures through an audiovisual mapping is provided to enable the understanding of complex 
musical pieces, such as operas and concertos. Real-time visual and gestural feedback is generated for the 
conductor and audience through the display of a set of particle emitters and the trajectory traversed by the 
captured gestures. Focusing on delivering a better user experience (UX), the system was also evaluated by 
professional conductors, musicians and users without any formal musical knowledge. UX played an important 
part in identifying that this type of experience is viable as an additional support for conducting and musical 
theory learning.   
1. Introduction 
There is a potential for immersive tools that can teach some basic 
aspects of conducting in a constructive way. Actually, several interactive 
conducting systems, which allow users to control tempo and dynamics, 
have been built in the past. According to Sarasúa [1], the Radio Baton 
system, designed in the 1970s by Max Mathews, was one of the earliest 
conducting systems in the world. The Radio Baton used radio-frequency 
technology in the creation of two batons to control the execution and 
intensity of musical notes. 
The combination of image and sound used together and the evolution 
of the baton have been noticed in systems such as Personal Orchestra [2] 
and You’re the Conductor [3]. In these systems, the interactive musical 
conducting was achieved using a baton with an infrared light to deter­
mine its position in a 2D space. Various models of control devices have 
been developed over the years, like the Conductor’s Jacket [4], a device 
capable of detecting several body parameters (such as muscle tension 
and respiration) and of mapping these parameters directly to musical 
data. 
Gestural input devices such as Microsoft Kinect [5] and Leap Motion 
Controller [6] have enabled the development of graphical applications 
of gesture control at a much lower cost and without loss of precision, 
when compared to the previously mentioned devices [7,8]. Currently, 
and probably for the years to come, other aspects of paramount 
importance of gestural input devices is hygiene management. Since 
these devices do not come into contact with the user’s hands or face, 
they are much safer and easier to maintain when comparing to headsets 
and controllers, for example, which are often shared amongst people. 
While the Kinect device pales in comparison to the Leap Motion 
Controller regarding precision of fine hand movements [9–11] there is a 
tradeoff in favor of the freedom of movement of the user’s body when 
interacting with conducting systems, which benefits the Kinect device. 
In addition, the kinect’s software is capable of automatically calibrating 
the sensor based on application and the player’s physical environment 
[12], accommodating for the presence of obstacles. Although there are 
newer technologies available nowadays regarding motion sensing input 
devices, the Kinect device is still popular (more than 35 million units 
have been sold since its release, turning out to be the most advanced 
device available to consumers worldwide), specially in places where 
cutting edge technology gadgets must be imported and their costs can be 
☆This paper has been recommended for acceptance by William Swartout. 
* Corresponding author.

Entertainment Computing 
journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/entcom 
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.entcom.2020.100399 
Received 17 June 2019; Received in revised form 3 June 2020; Accepted 5 December 2020   


Entertainment Computing 37 (2021) 100399
2
far expensive for the average people. 
Interactive graphic applications with gestural control have been 
gaining more visibility, however, the correct identification of user’s 
gestures still proves to be a challenge [13,14], since a same gesture can 
be made in different time intervals and be represented by visible bodily 
actions with varied forms and styles, depending on who performs it [9]. 
In addition to the challenges in the area of gestural control, those 
related to the visual representation of musical structure (based on the 
preprocessing of the input data and on the design of graphic represen­
tations of the music) with a satisfactory expressive quality are also 
important to be considered. Many authors have been attempting to 
contribute to the field of gestural performance in interactive conducting 
systems, but still a lot of key problems wait for better solutions. More­
over, the potential of visualization of musical structures and their visual 
elements in 3D has not yet been fully explored [15]. 
This work presents a Kinect-based conducting prototype system as a 
potential immersive tool which includes gestural control for educational 
purpose, a stylized visualization of an orchestra and musical structure, 
and a user experience (UX) evaluation of the system. Basically, the 
primary goal our tool is to teach some basic aspects of conducting in a 
constructive way, by enabling students to study music performance with 
emphasis in instrumental conducting, without the physical presence of 
an orchestra or choir. For instance, students can proactively pursue their 
learning goals by exercising the sense of tempo and ensuring correct 
entries by musical instruments. In addition, an audiovisual mapping is 
also provided to enable the understanding of more complex musical 
pieces, such as operas and concertos, through the visualization of 
melodic and harmonic structures. 
It extends significantly our previous work [15] by incorporating the 
user’s ability to control some aspects of the musical execution and its 
visual representation through typical conducting gestures using both 
hands, the music data processing of a great number of instruments 
playing simultaneously, the mapping and modeling of a choir, a new set 
of particle’s effects to represent the instruments’ sounds, and a new 
visual modeling for the representation of particle emitters’ of each or­
chestra section. Finally, focusing on delivering a better UX, our tool was 
also evaluated by professional conductors, musicians and users without 
any formal musical knowledge. 
2. Related work 
In terms of interaction within a virtual space and capturing hand- 
tracking information (two essential elements for conducting systems), 
typical VR systems and AR platforms comprise different graphical user 
interfaces that vary according to the level of immersion required, from 
HMDs to gestural control devices. 
Although HMDs can increase the user’s immersive experience, they 
have also several drawbacks. Moreover, they may be impractical in the 
educational setting since they are generally more expensive or more 
technically complex devices. Other concerns are the possible side effects 
of HMDs, such as simulator sickness, visual fatigue and, more recently, 
their hygiene management, which depending on the health scenario 
may also present a barrier to their wider educational use. Gestural 
control devices have also their limitations, but are much more afford­
able, rarely cause motion sickness, and allow users to interact with the 
application through simple contact-free hand gestures. 
Within this context, The Virtual Orchestra created by Ilmonen [16] 
presents a 3D graphic simulation of an orchestra controlled by a baton, 
which is tracked using accelerometers with six degrees-of-freedom to 
measure movements. On the downside, however, the accelerometers 
commonly generate measurement inaccuracies that cause drift over time 
(due to an integration formula which is used to calculate velocity and 
position values), requiring the development of application-specific 
signal processing solutions. Contrastingly, Lim and Yeo [8] created a 
smartphone-based music conducting system, named vMaestro, which 
uses the iPhone’s gyroscope (pitch-axis) as a tracking device. The user’s 
gestures control tempo and cueing for different instruments. 
Previous works have focused on recognizing a conducting gesture by 
relying on evaluating patterns of strong and weak downbeats of qua­
ternary and binary gestures [8,17]. Those works use a static image of an 
orchestra with three sections highlighted according to the direction 
faced by the conductor. 
Although originally designed for recognising large body movements, 
the Kinect device has been used by Toh et al. [18] to capture delicate 
conducting signals in a real-time interactive conducting system. Salgian 
et al. [19] also use the Microsoft Kinect on the Conducting Tutor, a 
system aimed to provide real time feedback about personal performance 
to beginner conducting students. Basically, it focuses only on tempo 
control and posture correction (such as swaying, rocking and excessive 
moving) during the execution of a musical piece. On the other hand, it is 
nicely refined to the point of classifying articulation as legato or 
staccato. 
More recently, the works of Han et al. [20], Pellegrini et al. [21] and 
Carthen et al. [22] rely on machine learning for gesture recognition. 
However, despite great potential, currently machine learning methods 
still rely on features selection, data preparation and training, noise 
reduction in the depth images acquired, etc. [23]. 
In this work, unlike many published works on interactive conducting 
systems with touchless interface using Kinect sensor [24,19], our solu­
tion allows the system dynamically react to the musical tempo changes. 
Similarly to the work described by Toh et al. [18], ours presents features 
such as start and stop music gestures, tempo control, volume adjust­
ments and instrument emphasis. While the gesture recognition solution 
proposed by those authors only emphasizes detecting the first and last 
beat trajectory as hints of a conducting gesture execution, ours also 
recognizes the whole gesture pattern. In addition to the features 
implemented by Lim et al. [8], our work includes ternary and compound 
binary patterns and presents a real-time visual, auditory and gestural 
system. Our MIDI-based sound output is similar to MUSE’s MIDI-based 
generation system [22]. MIDI is also used to play the music on Able­
ton Live [25], which in turn controls and plays the music with virtual 
instruments. Authors such as Lim et al. [8] evaluate patterns of strong 
and weak downbeats, while Chen et al. [26] use a “divide to conquer” 
approach by cutting a gesture into a set of elementary units corre­
sponding to specific commands and variations of gesture execution. In a 
different manner, and inspired by the design ideas of Caramiaux et al. 
about new forms of improving the expressivity during gestural perfor­
mance in interactive systems [27], our solution uses a path-tracking for 
each conducting gesture. Finally, unlike machine learning methods, our 
system is independently of prior data treatment. 
3. System overview 
Since music can be described as a collection of highly organized 
sounds and silences with varying structures in the tonal and temporal 
domains, it is a considerable challenge to define a visual representation 
capable of assisting users in understanding the structural elements of a 
musical composition, as well as its theme and variations. The scope of 
musical data that can be analyzed in a piece is too broad, thus, in this 
work, we opted to narrow it down to three identifiable sound charac­
teristics: pitch, intensity and timbre. Pitch indicates the highness or 
lowness of a sound (what notes are being played); intensity indicates the 
loudness or softness of a pitch; and timbre indicates what instrument is 
producing the sound [28]. 
By identifying when a specific sound occurs, its pitch, how loud it is, 
and what instrument is playing it, our system provides the user with a 
visual representation of melody, harmony, rhythm and dynamics. 
Focusing on the possible visual choices that could be made to 
represent the captured musical data in a way simpler than the traditional 
musical notation and on how the musical and visual performance could 
be affected by the recognized gestures, we designed the main compo­
nents of our system (Fig. 1), which is based on three types of input data: 
J.H. Fonteles and M.A. Formico Rodrigues                                                                                                                                                                                                


Entertainment Computing 37 (2021) 100399
3
a MIDI file that defines when each instrument starts and stops playing a 
specific note, a file that contains the standard conducting gestures, and 
data captured by the Kinect device representing the conductor’s gestures 
(which control the music’s tempo and dynamics, i.e., the variation in 
both speed and loudness, respectively). 
The MIDI Processor analyzes the Note events from the input file, 
mapping the extracted musical data to their visual representations in the 
Particle System Manager which, in turn, synchronizes the audio and 
visual outputs through the MIDI Interface and Renderer, respectively. 
On its turn, the Kinect 4 Unreal plugin [29] is constantly capturing data 
from the Kinect device and sending it to the Gesture Identifier. It uses the 
Gesture Tracker VR’s recognition algorithm, which is based on the work 
of Caramiaux et al. [27], to compare the captured data to the previously 
loaded conducting gestures. If there is a match, the Gesture Identifier 
maps the collected data to their visual representations in the Particle 
System Manager, effectively changing the whole output (the audio 
played through the speakers and the visual effects that appear on 
screen). 
3.1. Gestural control 
The gestures tracked by the Kinect sensor were accessed and visu­
alized using the Kinect 4 plugin for Unreal Engine [29]. At each tick of 
the animation, the “body” of the person located at the center of the 
device’s field of view is mapped. From this shape are extracted the ab­
solute positions of two joints (i.e., from the right and left hands), which 
are mapped to two static 3D meshes positioned in the scene. 
Three types of gestural control have been implemented in the pro­
totype: (1) diagrams of conducting performed with the right hand 
(shown in Fig. 2); (2) indications of prominent sections of instruments 
performed with the left hand; and (3) execution control of the selected 
music using both hands. 
Basically, the path traced by the user’s gestures is divided into small 
segments. Considering the movement of the user’s hand has the same 
direction as the current segment (i.e., if the dot product between the 
vectors of the tracked movement and of the current segment of the 
reference gesture is greater than a pre-established acceptable similarity 
threshold), the next segment is evaluated, as if the user’s hand was 
moving according to its expected path. If the gesture is successfully 
performed until the last segment, a recognition flag is set to “true”. All 
gestures are stored with the normalized z-rotation at 0. This allows to 
perform the same gesture in different directions (the direction is inter­
preted using the rotations of the Gesture Tracker VR system component). 
3.2. Visual appearance mapping 
In this work, three sound characteristics are extracted from each 
note: pitch, intensity and timbre. These elements and the paths gener­
ated by moving the user’s hands are represented visually on screen of the 
output device. 
Pitch: It is mapped on screen as the color of a spawned particle in the 
3D scene. During the musical performance, pitches vary and so do the 
particle’s colors accordingly. 
Intensity: Our work relates intensity to a particle’s size, so when the 
conductor performs a gesture that is larger than expected, the music’s 
loudness increases which, in turn, makes the spawned particles grow. 
Timbre: Since timbre is affected by the shape and material of an 
instrument, we modeled this element as shapes of 3D objects (particle 
emitters) representing each section of the orchestra/choir. 
The variation of particle’s colors follows the color mapping devel­
oped by Louis Bertrand Castel [30] in 1734, shown in Fig. 3. Although 
there are a number of mapping schemes that consider half-tones, we 
opted for Castel’s model because it was the first to establish different 
colors for each of the twelve existing musical notes, including flats (half- 
tone decreases to a note) and sharps (half-tone increases to a note). 
Fig. 1. The main components of the system.  
Fig. 2. Four basic conducting pattern diagrams (images generated by 
the authors). 
Fig. 3. On the top and bottom, respectively, Newton’s and Castel’s color maps 
(remade by the authors, adapted from [30]). 
J.H. Fonteles and M.A. Formico Rodrigues                                                                                                                                                                                                


Entertainment Computing 37 (2021) 100399
4
While modeling the appearance of the particle emitters, in this work 
we considered the different sections of an orchestra: String, Brass, 
Woodwind, Percussion and Voices. String instruments have similar 
shapes varying only in size, therefore, all of them are represented therein 
by the same violin 3D object. Most of the Brass instruments can be 
defined as metal tubes with bell-shaped ends, hence, their representa­
tion is an inverted bell end. The majority Woodwind instruments have 
elongated bodies, which inspired us to use cylindrical shapes to repre­
sent them. On the other hand, percussion instruments come in different 
shapes and sizes, making it difficult to choose a unique shape to repre­
sent them all. Since the drum is an instrument commonly associated 
with the notion of percussion and rhythm, a 3D drum object was used to 
represent all percussion instruments. For the visual representation of the 
voices, the female voices were differentiated from the male ones using 
objects aesthetically compatible with the modeled 3D scene. In partic­
ular, we have adapted the Zeycus’ abstract chess pieces of the queen and 
king [31] for creating these 3D representations. 
The choice of a particle system for this virtual representation was due 
to its geometric simplicity, as well as the fluidity and dynamics of the 
elements, which present a wide and rich variety of behaviors. These 
characteristics are fundamental, since a musical work has subtle nu­
ances, stylistic interpretations and different structural patterns. 
3.3. Extraction and control of musical data 
To capture the messages that determine which musical data need to 
be played, we had to define the input and output of MIDI data, creating a 
synthesizer in charge of playing the notes effectively. Each MIDI event 
that hits the sequencer is analyzed: in the case of a Note-on message, the 
three sound variables (pitch, intensity and timbre) are extracted, map­
ped and sent to the system’s renderer to update the visual attributes of 
the particles; and, in the case of a Note-off message, the emitter stops 
spawning new particles. Note-on and Note-off messages are followed by 
two bytes: the first byte represents the pitch of the note, while the second 
one provides information about its intensity. Velocity is the term used to 
define the intensity of the touch that generates sound: if it is strong, 
consequently the sound is very loud; whereas, if it is weak, the sound is 
low, as illustrated in Fig. 4. 
Taking into account that data input is a preexisting MIDI file, the 
system extracts all MIDI data from the Note-on and Note-off events and 
places them in a sequential list, which also includes: the timestamp, i.e., 
a sequence of characters that indicates the moment at which a certain 
event occurs; its respective instrument, through the channel and track 
attributes; its musical note, determined by the pitch attribute; and its 
intensity, represented by the velocity attribute, which indicates the force 
used to play the note [32]. The file is mapped so that the tracks and their 
respective instruments, selected in the sound bank, are identified. We 
also extract the resolution and duration of the song to be used when 
some change in the playback speed happens, due to a change in dy­
namics derived from a gesture execution. 
3.4. Instruments and sections 
Musical instruments can be split up into different groups or sections. 
Basically, these relate to the way an instrument is played, the sound it 
makes or the material it is made from. The main five groups are: (1) 
Strings, (2) Brass, (3) Woodwind, (4) Keyboards and (5) Percussion. In 
addition, the human voice is a natural musical instrument singing by 
people (alone or in groups) [33]. The human voice is essentially a wind 
instrument, with the lungs supplying the air, the vocal cords setting up 
the vibrations, and the cavities of the upper throat, mouth, and nose 
forming a resonating chamber. In our visualization system, we focus on 
five different sections of musical instruments (Strings, Brass, Woodwind, 
Percussion and Human Voices), as shown in Fig. 5, as the main char­
acters of the animations generated by a timeline particle system control. 
In particular, the Keyboard section has not been included in this 
work because it does not feature prominently in works prior to the 20th 
Century. In addition, it has been noticed that the discussion about the 
piano being considered a percussion or string instrument has been 
triggering controversial ideas, since opinions on this issue slightly differ. 
Nevertheless, we plan to address this issue in future work. 
4. User experience study and results 
Within the context of musical conducting gestures, even in a tradi­
tional usability testing, recruiting the right participants (professional 
conductors, musicians and ordinary users) can be quite a challenge. 
For a comparative usability test with other state of the art systems 
(focused on evaluating two or more conducting systems), participants 
selection can be even more difficult since there are many important 
additional variables to consider, including prior experience, domain 
skills and frequency of using those skills. Thus, testing many solutions 
becomes too complex for testers and the participants, because of the 
Fig. 4. Visual representation of the intensity of the touch: “weak” on the left 
and “strong” on the right. 
Fig. 5. Musical instruments and the five sections modelled in the system.  
J.H. Fonteles and M.A. Formico Rodrigues                                                                                                                                                                                                


Entertainment Computing 37 (2021) 100399
5
additional variables that must be controlled for in comparative testing. 
These variables span planning, execution, and test analysis, adding 
complexity to define and recruit test participants, create the test envi­
ronments, train usability test participants, execute test sessions and 
analyze test data, among others. In addition, the more systems you test, 
generally, the fewer task flows you can include in a study. 
Therefore, due to the complexity of doing a comparative usability 
test in this field, we focus on a traditional usability testing to build a set 
of functional assessments of our system components to firstly determine 
their ability to represent, respond to and process music and gestures, as 
well as to evaluate the implemented system elements. 
We describe the gestural controls performed using the Kinect device 
and the UX testing made with professional conductors, musicians (no 
conductors) and ordinary users, as a source of inspiration for system 
improvement and identification of its main limitations. More specif­
ically, this section is divided as follows: Musical and Functional Analysis 
and UX (including Participants and Method, Quantitative Analysis and 
Qualitative Analysis). 
4.1. Musical and functional analysis 
The traditional way of learning music is through the study of a 
musical score (Fig. 6), which contains objective notations of a musical 
composition. However, beginners usually spend considerable time 
learning the basics of musical theory before being able to understand 
these notations. 
In particular, a professional conductor must have advanced knowl­
edge for understanding the musical notations, which generally are 
specific of each instrument. There are several difficulties related spe­
cifically to pitch, since not every instrument uses the same clef (a 
reference symbol indicating where a certain note should be placed on 
the staff), such as the string section where the violin, viola and violon­
cello notes are written using treble, alto and bass clefs, respectively. 
Another difficulty is the existence of transposing instruments, in which 
the notes of the score do not directly correspond to the produced sounds, 
since they undergo transposition. 
Fig. 6 shows a page from the score of the Fourth Movement of 
Beethoven’s Ninth Symphony. One can observe that there is a great deal 
of information about each instrument, so that the conductor can follow 
everything that should happen in the correct passage execution. This 
page lasts less than 20 s, but the conductor spends a lot longer studying 
every note, pause and phrase of each instrument, as well as their dy­
namic markings. 
The mapping between color and pitch helps in understanding which 
note each instrument is playing. Different clefs or transposing in­
struments cause no problem, since a specific note in our system will 
always be visually represented with the same color. Fig. 6 has two areas 
highlighted with green color showing that although there are two 
trombones (alto and bass) their notes are written in different clefs. Since 
each type of clef assigns its reference note to a line, the different trom­
bone clefs make possible to identify that despite the different positions 
of the notes, shown in the smaller area highlighted in green, they 
actually correspond to the same E note. This moment is represented on 
the top right of the animation frame from the system’s execution, dis­
played in Fig. 7, showing the trombones emitting yellow particles that 
represent the E note. 
Another area highlighted (in red) in Fig. 6 shows that at a certain 
point in the performance, all the instruments are emitting sounds since 
there are notes in their staffs, except the four voices (soprano, alto, tenor 
and bass). The verification of pauses between the emission of notes is 
more clearly perceived by the absence or discontinuity of the particle 
emission (Fig. 8). 
Fig. 9 shows the emphasis given by the left hand with the increase of 
light from the emitters of the selected section. In addition, the change in 
amplitude and the subsequent increase in particles size is shown in 
Fig. 10. 
4.2. User Experience (UX) 
UX focuses on having a better understanding of users, what they 
need, what they value, and their abilities and difficulties, in order to 
identify the main limitations of the system, revise them, and improve the 
quality of the application and, consequently, the quality of user’s 
interaction. 
4.2.1. Participants and method 
UX testing was done to collect qualitative and quantitative data with 
participants without any formal musical knowledge and with profes­
sional musicians (including conductors). 
Participated in the UX and usability studies 18 users, being 14 
women and 4 men, aged between 20 and 78 years, with 6 of them having 
previous experience with gesture control devices when playing games. 
Among the 18 participants, 9 had no formal knowledge about music. In 
the group with the remaining 9 participants, all were musicians with 
continuous professional performing experience of at least 8 years, 
among which there were 3 conductors (aged 49, 56 and 78). In 
Fig. 6. Score of the Ninth Symphony.  
Fig. 7. Animation frame showing the emitters of the two trombones, gener­
ating particles of the same color (although they have different score markings). 
J.H. Fonteles and M.A. Formico Rodrigues                                                                                                                                                                                                


Entertainment Computing 37 (2021) 100399
6
particular, all professional conductors from our test group have over 20 
years of experience, directing choirs and orchestras. The remaining 
professional musicians have reported having also prior conducting 
knowledge. Therefore, it was possible to create three groups of partici­
pants: (1) Group 1, with professional conducting participants; (2) Group 
2, with musicians whose main specialties did not include conducting; 
and (3) Group 3, with participants without any formal musical 
knowledge. 
The main objective of the evaluation tasks was to verify the ability of 
the participants to perform the modeled gestures in isolation. In addi­
tion, another aim was to compare the performance (time spent, precision 
and number of false positives) of users without musical experience to the 
one of professional musicians, with a special interest in observing the 
experience of the conductors. 
Initially, calibration was performed to establish the range of gestural 
amplitude recognized by the system prototype, based on the gestures 
done by each participant. The participants were trained during 15 min, 
allowing for the execution of all tasks more than once, paying particular 
attention to the difference in the amplitude and velocity of the execution 
of the conducting gestures. Table 1 shows the eleven tasks done by each 
of the 18 participants through gestural control. 
The learning UX focuses on creating enhanced experience in a virtual 
space capable of enabling users to practice conducting without the 
actual need of the physical presence of an orchestra or choir. An iterative 
practice that starts with a simple task is used and gets refined with each 
successive task. More specifically, Tasks 1, 2, and 3 teach basic con­
ducting gesture skills, i.e., how to start and stop the music, as well as to 
choose a specific musical instrument or voice sections. The level of 
complexity of each task depends on the number of movements needed to 
perform each conducting gesture, which are listed to the user in 
ascending order as follows: Task 4 - Binary (2 movements), Task 5 - 
Ternary (3 movements), Task 6 - Quaternary (4 movements) and Task 7 - 
Compound Binary (6 movements). Once users develop skills and confi­
dence in their own learning abilities they can perform Tasks 8, 9, 10, and 
11, which in turn require to use the skills and knowledge learned from 
Task 6 (when performing the quaternary gesture). 
4.2.2. Quantitative analysis 
To perform the quantitative analysis, for each individual participant, 
the time spent and the required number of attempts to complete each of 
the eleven tasks were measured and recorded. Tables 2 and 3 respec­
tively show, for each of the eleven tasks per group, the average time 
spent in seconds and the median number of attempts to complete them. 
Regarding the average time per group for the execution of Task 1, in 
row 1 of Table 2 is shown a discrepancy between the group’s perfor­
mance, with a standard deviation of ±1.38s among the data values. 
More specifically, participants from Group 3, approximately, spent 75% 
more time to complete Task 1 than participants from Group 1. In spite of 
this, only 1 to 2 attempts (median values shown in row 1 of Table 3) 
were needed to complete this task. 
Nine out of 18 participants were able to perform Task 2 in a single 
attempt and, on average, spent around 2.5 s to do it. As regards the time 
and attempts per group, one can note a better users’ performance, when 
comparing to the results achieved in Task 1. This was probably due to 
now-more familiar relative hand’s position with respect to the Kinect 
device. 
Fig. 8. Animation frame showing the voice emitters turned off, since in the 
score there is a rest. The path followed by the conductor’s gesture is also 
visualized on screen in real-time during system execution. 
Fig. 9. Animation frame with the particle emitters on the left highlighted.  
Fig. 10. Animation frame showing the increase of particles size, in relation to 
the amplitude of the executed gesture. The path followed by the conductor’s 
gesture is also visualized on screen in real-time during system execution.

On average, regarding Task 3, participants from Group 1 and 2 had a 
similar performance (12.55 and 12.75 s, respectively), with a small 
standard deviation of ±0.14s, better than the one from Group 3 (13.91 s). 
This behavior may be due to the prior knowledge of the conductors and 
musicians with regard to the position of instruments and voices in an 
orchestra. Also, a median value of two attempts was necessary for all 
groups to perform Task 3 successfully, which probably is related to the 
non-triviality in recognizing the gesture of selecting instruments’ groups 
with the Kinect device. This gesture requires not only moving the left 
hand up, down and to the sides, but also stretching the arm forward to 
select groups of instruments located far away in the orchestra. 
Tasks 4, 5, 6 and 7 were performed without musical accompaniment 
to exclusively evaluate the correctness of the conducting gestures (bi­
nary, ternary, quaternary, and compound binary, respectively), 
regardless the execution’s speed. As expected, the participants from 
Group 1 (conductors) performed each of the gestures more quickly and in 
fewer attempts than the others, followed by those from Group 2 (musi­
cians). In particular, testers perceived the occurrence of false positives 
between the execution of binary and ternary gestures in 18% of the 
cases. 
Among the four gestures, the quaternary, despite its greater 
complexity (when compared to the binary and ternary), in general 
required fewer attempts to be recognized. It has been performed on the 
first attempt by participants from each of the three groups, which leads 
us to believe that, probably, tracing the four directions in the shape of a 
cross made this conducting movement more stable and familiar to all 
participants. 
Regarding the three groups, the compound binary was the most 
difficult gesture to perform and be recognized, presenting the worst 
performance times and the largest number of attempts, as shown in row 
7 of Table 2. The great disparity between the average performance times 
of participants from Group 1 and the others was particularly already 
expected for this gesture. However, musicians (Group 2), on average, 
needed approximately two times more time than the conductors (Group 
1) to complete Task 7, with performance results more closer to the ones 
showed by ordinary users. One possible reason for this might be the fact 
that musicians, who are not specialized in conducting, usually study the 
binary, ternary and quaternary gestures, but not the compound binary 
one. 
For Tasks 8, 9, 10, and 11, we have used the excerpt from the Fourth 
Movement of Beethoven’s Ninth Symphony, known as “Ode to Joy” 
[34], conducted with a quaternary gesture, since these tasks involved 
both changing musical parameters and animating 3D scene graphical 
elements of instruments/voices. More specifically, as regards the 
average execution time for Task 8 per group, a huge disparity between 
participants from Groups 1 and 3 is shown in row 8 of Table 2, providing 
evidence of the specificity of this task in the conducting domain. 
Regarding Task 9, the average performance times per group were greater 
than the ones spent to complete Task 8. This was probably due to the 
own nature of Task 9, which required the participants to perform a 
gesture of greater amplitude, due to a great slowness in the music 
execution. 
Regarding how many attempts participants from Groups 1 and 3 
made for completing Tasks 8 and 9, the results were identical (shown in 
rows 8 and 9, and columns 2 and 4, respectively, of Table 3), that is, no 
matter performing the decrease or increase loudness gesture, conductors 
needed 2 attempts, while ordinary users needed 3. Participants from 
Groups 2 (musicians) and 3 (ordinary users) required the same amount of 
attempts for Task 8. 
During Task 10, participants had to perform the quaternary gesture 
very slowly, to slow down tempo. Again, musicians and ordinary users 
had similar performances (11.24 and 10.46 s, respectively), which were 
considerably worse than the professional conductors’ one (i.e., 6.43 s). A 
similar behavior was observed regarding the number of attempts, as 
shown in row 10, columns 2, 3, and 4 of Table 3. 
The average time per group to perform Task 11 (slow down tempo of 
quaternary gesture) was considerably shorter than for concluding Task 
10 (speed up tempo of quaternary gesture). It is worth mentioning that 
Task 11 demanded to perform the gesture more quickly, which explains 
its much lower average time values. Finally, in most of the tasks, Group 1 
had the best performance, followed by Group 2 and, finally, Group 3. 
4.2.3. Qualitative analysis 
According to Garret [35], in order to have a meaningful and valuable 
user experience, the system information must be, among others char­
acteristics: useful (content should be original and fulfill a need), usable 
(it must be easy to use) and desirable (image, identity, and other design 
elements are strategic to evoke emotion and appreciation). 
Therefore, after completing the tests, an extra evaluation form with 
subjective items was applied to the professional conductors to provide 
more detailed feedback about their personal perceptions, particularly, 
regarding the gestural controls and their visual representations on 
screen. 
The three conductors stated that it was possible to perform the 
conducting gestures in the planned way and that the system’s mapped 
gestures were mostly similar to those performed when conducting an 
orchestra or choir in real life. 
In regard to the visual mapping of the instruments, they reported that 
the placement of instruments is faithful to what is commonly seen in an 
orchestra, and praised the visual effects representing each section of 
instruments. The conductors also appreciated the modeling of the voi­
ces, particularly, in respect to their positioning, visual appearance and 
distinction between the effects mapped for the female and male voices. 
Two conductors (from Group 1) expressed that during the gestures’ 
execution, the path followed by their hands (rendered on the application 
screen and shown at the bottom of Figs. 7 and 10) visually polluted the 
3D scene. They suggested to remove this graphic element from the 
interface to make it clearer. On the other hand, most participants of 
Group 3 noted this graphic element and, instead of having it removed, 
they suggested to render it differently, i.e., as a larger and brighter 
gesture path to help users to easily confirm the correctness of their 
conducting actions. 
When evaluating all the eleven tasks, the conductors highlighted the 
ease of execution for most of them. In addition, they mentioned the use 
of the left hand movement as an interesting educational resource to give 
emphasis to a specific section. They also noted that after correctly 
executing a gesture, the application’s response time seemed to be 
delayed on some occasions. This probably happened because the current 
version of the system can only execute a change in the visual and audio 
outputs after recognizing the complete gesture; whereas in real life, 
musicians perceive the conductor’s changes in each beat that forms a 
conducting gesture. 
4.3. Final discussion 
The main benefits along with the limitations of our work are briefly 
discussed in this section.

J.H. Fonteles and M.A. Formico Rodrigues                                                                                                                                                                                                


Entertainment Computing 37 (2021) 100399

Benefits: UX with different types of users (professional conductors, 
musicians, and users without any formal musical knowledge) played an 
important part in identifying that this type of virtual experience is viable 
as an additional support for conducting and musical theory learning, 
while at the same time proved that the system is effective, simple and 
flexible. UX studies have also shown that the understanding of a complex 
musical piece can be possible through the visualization of melodic and 
harmonic structures using an audiovisual mapping, as a representation 
of the emitted sounds. Without the physical presence of an orchestra or 
choir, professional conductors have also reported a sense of belief when 
virtually conducting the score of the Fourth Movement of the Ninth 
Symphony by Beethoven. Moreover, the participants stressed that a very 
positive aspect of the application is that this type of gestural, visual and 
musical experience can expose users to diverse learning of musical 
structures, offering freedom to repeat and return to the music execution 
and visualization analysis, as many times as needed. Furthermore, a 
direct relationship has been found between the success of the tasks and 
the level of musical knowledge and conducting among the participants. 
Despite the profile variation of the individuals in each of the groups, 
regarding the number of attempts to perform the gestures and the time 
required to complete the tasks, none of the participants considered these 
impossible tasks and some of them found them easy. In the short future, 
applications and uses of different types of immersive tools can be 
envisioned and probably will be increasingly common to be found. 
Limitations: A limitation of the current system is the occurrence of 
false positives in the gestural recognition of the binary and ternary 
rhythms. In addition, by watching carefully how real conductors, mu­
sicians and users without any formal musical knowledge engaged with 
our prototype interface, we could identify usability problems and gaps in 
the user flows, and gain other valuable insights that might have other­
wise gone unnoticed. In this case, one possible idea is to conceive an 
alternative gesture recognition solution capable to better represent the 
conductor’s change in pace or amplitude prior to the gesture being 
completed (tracking individual beats for each gesture). Finally, taking 
into account the participants’ feedback regarding the experienced 
slowness between the executed gesture and the animation response 
time, one possibility to overcome this limitation would be to identify 
where the process may be faced with continuity loss (e.g., due to a visual 
processing overload or problems with the device’s data capture, or even 
in the data manipulation and mapping). 
5. Conclusions and future work 
Despite being an active research area in computer vision, hand 
gesture recognition for human computer interaction is still a real-world 
problem difficult to solve. In addition, it is paramount for a conductor to 
develop their style and that comes with experience, conducting a variety 
of pieces, trying different stylistic choices, and learning from other 
players, different choirs and orchestras. Thus, replacing these pro­
fessionals by gestural systems is out of the question, since their 
uniqueness is a fundamental part of the success of a conductor. 
On the other hand, a computer-assisted learning tool can be useful 
for novice musicians to retain some aspects of conducting in a 
constructive way when an orchestra or choir is not readily available to 
them. In this work, we presented a Kinect-based conducting prototype 
system that uses gestural control as a small step towards a tool for 
educational purposes. Our UX testing focused on having an under­
standing of users (professional conductors, musicians, and users without 
any formal musical knowledge), what they need, what they value, their 
abilities, and also their limitations when interacting with the system by 
experiencing different forms of visual feedback for musical performance 
through conducting gestures (binary, ternary, quaternary, and com­
pound binary) with the right hand; controlling music execution with 
both hands; and selecting different groups of instruments and voices 
using the left hand. Our system has a potential for an immersive tool to 
support basic aspects of conducting learning, particularly, which can be 
used to study the score, practice some gestures, go through difficult 
sections, become familiar with a composer’s intentions so that when 
rehearsing the piece with other musicians, the muscle memory (in which 
memories for movement patterns are acquired through repetition [36]) 
will be sharper and the conductor’s focus will be on reading body lan­
guage, eye contact, energy, and on reacting accordingly, shaping the 
real sound. 
As future work, initially we plan to mitigate the already mentioned 
limitations of this study. As for gestural recognition, more robust 
methods can be explored, especially in the area of Machine Learning 
[37,38]. Other possibilities are the improvement of current gesture 
recognition accuracy and the identification of individual differences in 
the production and perception of gestures [39]. Newer hand-tracking 
technologies can also be used, such as Oculus Quest for hand-tracking, 
Augmented Reality platforms for understanding of hand movements 
and gestures, DepthKit for more accurate yet abstract representation of a 
volumetric space that is responsive to a user, and immersive audio. 
Finally, there is a plan to perform a more in-depth data analysis for the 
refinement of this work in the long run. Another possibility to consider 
would be to do a comparative study with other state-of-the-art con­
ducting systems, however, this will ultimately depend on many other 
important challenging variables already presented therein.
